text: The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 (RPWD Act) is India's landmark disability legislation enacted on December 16, 2016. It received Presidential assent on December 27, 2016 and came into force on April 19, 2017 (Act 49 of 2016). It replaces the Persons with Disabilities Act of 1995.

The RPWD Act 2016 recognizes 21 specified disabilities, expanded from 7 under the 1995 Act. These include: Blindness, Low Vision, Leprosy Cured, Hearing Impairment (Deaf), Hard of Hearing, Speech & Language Disability, Locomotor Disability, Dwarfism, Intellectual Disability, Specific Learning Disabilities, Autism Spectrum Disorder, Mental Illness, Cerebral Palsy, Muscular Dystrophy, Chronic Neurological Conditions, Multiple Sclerosis, Haemophilia, Thalassemia, Sickle Cell Disease, Acid Attack Victims, and Parkinson's Disease.

Under RPWD Act 2016, Benchmark Disability means a person with not less than 40% of a specified disability as certified by a certifying authority. Only persons with benchmark disability are entitled to reservations in government jobs and higher education.

Employment Rights under RPWD Act 2016: Minimum 4% reservation in government vacancies for persons with benchmark disabilities (increased from 3% in 1995 Act). Breakdown: 1% each for persons with blindness/low vision; deaf/hard of hearing; locomotor disability/cerebral palsy/leprosy cured/dwarfism/acid attack victims; and 1% for persons with intellectual/mental illness/multiple disabilities.

Higher Education Reservation under RPWD Act 2016: Minimum 5% seats reserved for persons with benchmark disabilities in all government educational institutions and institutions receiving government aid. Students also receive scholarships and fee waivers.

Blindness (Visual Impairment Definition under RPWD Act 2016): Blindness is complete absence of sight. A person is considered legally blind if their vision is 6/60 (20/200) or worse in the better eye with correction, or their visual field is restricted to 20 degrees or less. Blindness can be congenital (from birth) or acquired due to injury, disease (cataract, glaucoma, retinitis pigmentosa, diabetic retinopathy), or aging. Common causes in India include cataracts, corneal scarring, retinal diseases, and optic nerve damage.

Low Vision (Visual Impairment Definition under RPWD Act 2016): Low vision means reduced visual acuity that cannot be corrected by glasses or surgery but preserves some functional vision. Defined as vision worse than 6/18 but not less than 6/60 (or 20/60 to 20/200) in the better eye with correction, or visual field restricted from 20-40 degrees. Persons with low vision can use magnification devices, large print materials, and modified lighting to read and navigate. Causes include macular degeneration, diabetic retinopathy, cataracts, and refractive errors.

Hearing Impairment (Deaf and Hard of Hearing Definition under RPWD Act 2016): Deafness is substantial/permanent loss of hearing resulting in inability to hear and understand sound and speech. Hard of Hearing refers to partial/fluctuating hearing loss not corrected by hearing aids, leading to difficulty in communication. Deaf and hard of hearing are separate disabilities recognized under RPWD Act. Hearing loss measured in decibels (dB): Normal (0-20dB), Mild (20-40dB), Moderate (40-60dB), Severe (60-80dB), Profound (>80dB). Causes include congenital absence, infections (meningitis, otitis media), aging, noise exposure, ototoxic drugs.

Speech and Language Disability (Definition under RPWD Act 2016): Permanent disability of speech and/or language affecting communication ability regardless of intelligibility. Includes: Dysarthria (speech motor control problems), Apraxia (speech planning disorder), Voice disorders (pitch/quality abnormalities), Fluency disorders (stammering/stuttering), Receptive/Expressive language disorders. Can result from neurological damage (stroke, cerebral palsy, Parkinson's), structural abnormalities (cleft palate), hearing loss, intellectual disability, or autism. Affects social participation, education, and employment.

Accessibility provisions under RPWD Act (Sections 40-46): All government buildings must be accessible within a prescribed timeframe. Accessible roads, transport and infrastructure required. All public websites must comply with accessibility standards (WCAG). Public and private service providers must comply with accessibility standards.

Rights under RPWD Act: Right to Equality and Non-Discrimination (Section 3). No discrimination on grounds of disability. Right to Life with Dignity. Protection from torture, cruel or inhuman treatment. Right to Community Inclusion. Reproductive Rights (right to decide family size, retain fertility). Right to Legal Capacity (Section 14). Right to Vote in accessible polling stations. Right to Own Property.

Education Rights under RPWD Act (Sections 16-18): Right to free and compulsory education in neighborhood schools. 5% reservation in higher education. Accommodation in examinations: extra time (minimum 20 minutes per hour), scribe facility, use of assistive devices. Barrier-free accessible school buildings. Appointment of Special Educators. Home-based education for those unable to attend school.

Accessible India Campaign (Sugamya Bharat Abhiyan): Launched in 2015 by Government of India. Aims to make built environment, transportation systems, and ICT ecosystem accessible for persons with disabilities. Targets: 50% government buildings fully accessible, all airports and railway stations accessible, all government websites WCAG 2.0 compliant.

ADIP Scheme (Assistance to Disabled Persons for Purchase/Fitting of Aids and Appliances): Free/subsidized assistive devices to PwDs below income threshold (Rs. 20,000/month). Devices include: wheelchairs, hearing aids, white canes, Braille kits, tricycles, prosthetic limbs, orthotic appliances, DAISY players for the blind, communication aids.

SIPDA (Scheme for Implementation of Persons with Disabilities Act): Provides financial assistance to state governments and NGOs for implementing RPWD Act. Funds accessible infrastructure, awareness programs, and disability-related services.

DDRS (Deendayal Disabled Rehabilitation Scheme): Financial support to NGOs providing rehabilitation services for persons with disabilities. Covers special schools, vocational training centers, early intervention centers, and halfway homes.

NHFDC (National Handicapped Finance and Development Corporation): Provides loans at concessional rates for self-employment and entrepreneurship by persons with disabilities. Loan amounts up to Rs. 30 lakh. Interest rates starting from 5% per annum.

Indira Gandhi National Disability Pension Scheme (IGNDPS): Monthly pension for below poverty line persons with disabilities. Amount: Rs. 300-500 per month (state governments may add additional amounts). Eligibility: BPL, age 18-79, 80% or more disability.

UDID (Unique Disability Identity Card / Swavalamban Card): National database of persons with disabilities. Provides standardized disability certificate. Apply at: www.swavlambancard.gov.in. Required for accessing government reservations and benefits. Contains embedded chip with disability information.

National Programme for Control of Blindness (NPCB): Free cataract surgeries, free glasses to school children with low vision, free treatment for corneal blindness. Vision centers at PHC level. Free IOL implants. Pre and post operative care. Objectives: eliminate avoidable blindness, reduce visual impairment burden, provide rehabilitation services. Website: npcb.nic.in

Special Benefits for Persons with Visual Impairment (Blindness/Low Vision) under RPWD Act 2016: Free Braille kits and Braille learning materials via ADIP scheme. Free white canes and orientation & mobility training. Priority in employment (1% quota in government jobs for blind/low vision). Free assistive devices including screen readers, DAISY players, magnification software. Tax exemptions on reading materials and books. Exemption from property/entertainment taxes in several states.

Education and Employment Support for Persons with Visual Impairment: NIRD provides training in Braille, orientation & mobility, daily living skills. IIT training programs for IT skills for blind persons. NIEDB (National Institute for Empowerment of Blind) at Dehradun offers rehabilitation training. Government jobs: 4% quota (1% for blind/low vision). 20 minutes extra time per hour in exams. Use of screen reader or scribe in examinations. Audio books and Braille materials provided free.

Free Assistive Technology and Rehabilitation for Visual Impairment: National Institute for the Visually Handicapped (NIVH), Dehradun provides free training in: Braille reading/writing, typing, computer skills with screen readers, orientation and mobility, rehabilitation services, low vision aids training. Free screen reader software: NVDA (open source), JAWS (subsidized for students). DAISY players for audio books. Free training provided across India through state centers.

NIEDB (National Institute for Empowerment of Blind/Low Vision Persons): Autonomous organization under Ministry of Social Justice working since 1989. Offers rehabilitation, vocational training, IT training, Braille education. Conducts research on visual impairment. Provides free counseling and guidance. Centers in Delhi, Kolkata, and regional programs. 100% scholarship for courses. Website: niedb.org. Email: niedb@niedb.org. Phone: 011-41591234

NPPCD (National Programme for Prevention and Control of Deafness): Free hearing aids through government hospitals and audiological and speech therapy centers. Newborn hearing screening. School hearing screening. Advanced hearing aids for severe/profound hearing loss.

National Trust Act 1999: For welfare of persons with Autism, Cerebral Palsy, Intellectual Disability and Multiple Disabilities. Key schemes: SAMARTH (care homes), GHARAUNDA (group homes for adults), NIRAMAYA (health insurance up to Rs. 1 lakh), PRERNA (early intervention), SAMBHAV (assistive device camps), VIKAAS (day care). Apply at: nationaltrust.gov.in

Samagra Shiksha Inclusive Education Component: Individualized Education Plans (IEPs) for children with disabilities. Resource rooms with therapy equipment. Home-based education for severely disabled children. Braille kits, hearing aids, and assistive devices. Orientation and mobility training. Rs. 3500 per child per year for disability-related needs.

Assistive Technology for Visual Impairment: Screen readers (NVDA - free, JAWS - paid, Voiceover for Apple). Braille displays and Braille printers. DAISY players for audio books. Magnification software (ZoomText, Windows Magnifier). White cane (provided free under ADIP scheme). Optical Character Recognition (OCR) software. Available through ADIP scheme and National Institute for Visually Handicapped, Dehradun.

Assistive Technology for Hearing Impairment: Hearing aids (BTE, ITE, RIC types - free under ADIP/NPPCD). Cochlear implants (government schemes for children). FM systems for classroom use. Video relay services and captioning. Sign language interpretation. Vibrating alert systems. TTY/TDD devices. AYJNIHH Mumbai provides free hearing aids.

Assistive Technology for Locomotor Disability: Wheelchairs (manual and motorized - free under ADIP). Prosthetic limbs (free at ALIMCO centers). Orthotic appliances (calipers, splints). Crutches and walking frames. Stair lifts and ramps. Modified vehicles (tax exemption available). Tricycles with hand pedals. Available through ALIMCO (Artificial Limbs Manufacturing Corporation of India).

Assistive Technology for Intellectual and Learning Disabilities: Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) devices. Symbol-based communication boards (low-tech). Speech generating devices (high-tech). Special education software (Boardmaker, LetMeTalk). Modified keyboards and mice. Text-to-speech software. Reading pens. Graphic organizers and visual schedules.

ALIMCO (Artificial Limbs Manufacturing Corporation of India): Government enterprise manufacturing and distributing assistive devices. Products: prosthetic limbs, orthotic appliances, wheelchairs, hearing aids, Braille kits, tricycles. Devices provided free/subsidized through ADIP scheme. Centers across India. Website: www.alimco.in

Accessibility Standards for Buildings (IS 9954:2020 / Harmonized Guidelines 2016): Ramps: minimum width 1.5m, slope 1:12. Accessible parking: minimum 2% of total spaces. Accessible toilets mandatory in all public buildings. Door width minimum 900mm. Lifts with Braille buttons and audio announcements. Tactile pathways from entrance to key facilities. Handrails on both sides of stairs.

Digital Accessibility Standards: All central government websites must comply with GIGW (Guidelines for Indian Government Websites) and WCAG 2.0 Level AA. Key requirements: alt text for images, keyboard navigation, sufficient color contrast (4.5:1 ratio), captions for videos, screen reader compatibility. NIC provides audit services.

Transport Accessibility in India: Indian Railways: accessible coaches in all trains, accessible toilets, reserved seats near entrance. Priority boarding for PwDs. Buses: CRPF mandates low-floor buses. Metro: all stations must have lifts, tactile tiles, audio announcements. Aviation: DGCA mandates accessible airports, wheelchair assistance free of charge, no extra fare for mobility aids.

WHO Global Disability Statistics (2023): 1.3 billion people (16% of world population) live with significant disability - world's largest minority group. 80% live in low and middle income countries. 46% of people aged 60+ have disability. 240 million children with disabilities worldwide. Persons with disabilities are 3x more likely to be denied healthcare.

India Disability Statistics (Census 2011): 26.8 million persons with disabilities (2.21% of population - likely underestimate). WHO estimates actual prevalence 10-20%. Distribution: Locomotor 20.3%, Hearing 18.9%, Vision 18.8%, Other 17.9%, Multiple 8.3%, Speech 7.5%, Intellectual 5.6%, Mental illness 2.7%. 69% in rural areas. Literacy rate 54.5% vs national 74%.

Examination Accommodations for Students with Disabilities in India: CBSE/UGC/UPSC provisions: Extra time (20 minutes per hour for benchmark disability). Scribe (writer) facility. Use of assistive devices (calculator for dyscalculia, magnifiers for low vision). Question papers in accessible formats (large print, Braille). Separate examination room. Compensatory time for those using scribes.

University Disability Cells (Equal Opportunity Cells): All universities must have Equal Opportunity Cells under RPWD Act. Functions: identify and enroll students with disabilities, ensure reasonable accommodations, resolve grievances, liaise with departments. Contact the Disability/Equal Opportunity Cell of your university for: fee concessions, accessible hostels, assistive devices, exam accommodations.

Grievance Redressal under RPWD Act: Chief Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities (national level) - File complaint at ccdisabilities.nic.in. State Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities (state level). Liaison Officers in every government establishment. High Courts/Supreme Court via writ petitions. National Human Rights Commission. Limitation: 1 year from date of grievance.

Legal Provisions for Employment Discrimination under RPWD Act: Sections 20-24. Equal Opportunity Policy mandatory for all establishments. Employer cannot discriminate in recruitment, promotion, or service conditions on grounds of disability. Penalty for contravention: fine up to Rs. 10,000 (first offence), Rs. 50,000-5 lakh (subsequent). File complaint with Liaison Officer or State/Chief Commissioner.

National Institutes for Disability Services in India: NIEPMD Chennai (Multiple Disabilities), SVNIRTAR Odisha (Locomotor), NILD Kolkata (Locomotor), NIHH Mumbai (Hearing), AYJNIHH Mumbai (Speech & Hearing), NIEPVD Dehradun (Visual), NIMHR Secunderabad (Intellectual), NIEPID Secunderabad (Intellectual Disability). All provide free/subsidized rehabilitation, training, and assistive devices.

Disability Certificate Process in India: Step 1: Visit government hospital with disability specialist. Step 2: Medical Board assesses disability type and percentage. Step 3: Certificate issued with UDID number. Step 4: Register on swavlambancard.gov.in for digital UDID card. Required documents: identity proof, address proof, medical reports. Processing time: 30-60 days. Valid for lifetime (some may require renewal).

Tax Benefits for Persons with Disabilities in India: Income Tax Act Section 80U: Deduction of Rs. 75,000 (40-80% disability) or Rs. 1,25,000 (severe disability 80%+) for persons with disability. Section 80DD: Deduction up to Rs. 1,25,000 for dependent with severe disability. GST exemption on assistive devices (wheelchairs, hearing aids, Braille watches). Customs duty exemption on imported assistive devices.

State Government Disability Schemes vary across India. Common benefits include: disability pension (Rs. 500-3000/month by state), free bus travel, free train travel (50-75% concession), free/subsidized housing under state schemes, subsidized loans, priority in BPL ration cards. Contact State Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities or State Welfare Department for state-specific schemes.

National Mission on Sickle Cell Anaemia (2023): Launched to address high burden among tribal populations. Free screening, counseling, and treatment. Targets 7 crore people in high-prevalence states. States covered: Madhya Pradesh, Rajasthan, Gujarat, Maharashtra, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, Odisha, Uttarakhand, Uttar Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, Assam, West Bengal.

CHAPTER 1: INTRODUCTION TO DISABILITY
1.1 Defining Disability
Disability is a complex and multidimensional phenomenon that reflects the interaction
between features of a person's body and the physical and social environment in which they
live. It is not merely a medical condition or an individual deficit, but rather a dynamic
interaction between health conditions and contextual factors – both personal and
environmental. The understanding of disability has evolved significantly over the past few
decades, shifting from a purely medical model to a biopsychosocial and human
rights-based model.
The World Health Organization (WHO), in its landmark World Report on Disability (2011),
defines disability as follows:
"Disability is part of the human condition. Almost everyone will be temporarily

or permanently " "impaired at some point in life, and those who survive to old
age will experience increasing " "difficulties in functioning. Most extended
families have a disabled member, and many non-disabled " "people take
responsibility for supporting and caring for their relatives and friends with
disabilities."
1.2 Models of Disability
Historically, disability has been understood through different conceptual models, each with
its own implications for policy and practice:
 Medical Model: Treats disability as a problem located within the individual, caused by
disease, trauma, or other health conditions. The focus is on 'curing' or 'normalizing' the
individual.
 Social Model: Views disability as created by the social and physical environment that

fails to accommodate people with impairments. Disability is a form of oppression
caused by social barriers.
 Biopsychosocial Model (WHO/ICF): Combines both medical and social perspectives.
Disability arises from the interaction between an individual's health condition and
contextual factors (environmental and personal). This is the basis of the International
Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF).
 Human Rights Model: Views people with disabilities as rights holders. Disability is a
matter of human rights, not charity. This model underpins the UN Convention on the
Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UNCRPD) and India's RPWD Act 2016.
1.3 The International Classification of Functioning (ICF)

The International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF), developed by
WHO in 2001, represents the most comprehensive framework for understanding disability.
It provides a standard language and framework for describing health and health-related
states. The ICF classifies disability across three dimensions:

1.4 Why Disability Matters: A Global Imperative
Disability is a global health and social issue of enormous magnitude. With approximately
1.3 billion people – roughly 16% of the world's population – living with some form of
disability, it represents the world's largest minority group. People with disabilities
experience disproportionate rates of poverty, social exclusion, and poor health outcomes.
Despite this, disability often remains invisible in national data systems, policy frameworks,
and development agendas. Addressing disability is therefore central to achieving the
Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and upholding universal human rights.

CHAPTER 2: WHO – GLOBAL DISABILITY
OVERVIEW
2.1 WHO's Role in Disability Policy
The World Health Organization plays a central role in shaping global disability policy,
providing technical guidance, generating evidence, and supporting countries to build
inclusive health systems. WHO's work on disability is organized under its Department of
Noncommunicable Diseases, Sensory Functions, Disability and Rehabilitation.
Key WHO milestones on disability include:
 1976: First International Classification of Impairments, Disabilities and Handicaps
(ICIDH) introduced.
 2001: International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF) published.
 2006: UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UNCRPD) adopted;
WHO was a key technical contributor.

 2011: World Report on Disability – the first comprehensive global analysis of disability,
published jointly by WHO and the World Bank.
 2015: WHO Global Disability Action Plan 2014–2021 launched to improve the health of
persons with disabilities.
 2021: WHO Rehabilitation 2030 Initiative to scale up rehabilitation services globally.
 2022: WHO Global Report on Health Equity for Persons with Disabilities published, the
most recent major WHO flagship report.
2.2 The WHO World Report on Disability (2011)
The World Report on Disability (WRD) was the first comprehensive global report on
disability. It was jointly produced by WHO and the World Bank, drawing on evidence from
over 100 countries. The report was launched at the United Nations headquarters in New

York on 9 June 2011 and received widespread media and policy attention globally.
The WRD examined disability across multiple domains, including healthcare, rehabilitation,
assistance and support, enabling environments, education, employment, and social
protection. It documented the circumstances of persons with disabilities across the world
and explored measures to promote their social participation.
Key Findings of the WRD (2011):
✓ Approximately 15% of the world's population – over 1 billion people – lives with
disability.
✓ Between 110 million and 190 million adults experience significant difficulties in
functioning.

✓ People with disabilities face significant barriers to accessing healthcare, education, and
employment.
✓ Disability prevalence is higher in developing countries.
✓ Women with disabilities are particularly vulnerable to discrimination and violence.
✓ Children with disabilities are less likely to attend school or receive healthcare.
✓ People with disabilities experience higher rates of poverty than non-disabled persons.
✓ Majority of barriers faced by persons with disabilities are avoidable.
2.3 WHO Global Report on Health Equity for Persons with
Disabilities (2022)
In December 2022, WHO released its most recent flagship publication on disability – the
Global Report on Health Equity for Persons with Disabilities. This report focused

specifically on health disparities and called for urgent action to close the health gap
between people with and without disabilities.
The 2022 report highlighted that persons with disabilities:
 Are twice as likely to have inadequate healthcare skills in their providers.
 Are nearly three times more likely to be denied healthcare.
 Are four times more likely to report being treated badly in healthcare settings.
 Are more than twice as likely to find health-care providers' skills inadequate.
 Experience 50% higher unmet healthcare needs compared to non-disabled persons.
 Are more likely to experience catastrophic out-of-pocket health expenditures.
 Face systematic exclusion from health promotion programs.

CHAPTER 3: WHO KEY STATISTICS &
EPIDEMIOLOGY
3.1 Global Prevalence
The scale of disability globally is staggering. According to the most recent WHO estimates
(2023), approximately 1.3 billion people – or 16% of the global population – experience
significant disability. This figure has grown significantly from WHO's earlier 1970s estimate
of 10%, and even from the 2011 estimate of 15%. This growth is attributed to aging
populations, the rise of chronic diseases, and improvements in measurement
methodologies.

3.3 Disability and Age

Disability is strongly correlated with age. The WHO estimates that approximately 46% of
people aged 60 years and older have some form of disability, compared to about 15% of
the general population. This pattern has major implications for healthcare systems as
global populations age rapidly, particularly in low- and middle-income countries.
3.4 Disability and Gender
Disability disproportionately affects women. Women with disabilities face double
discrimination – first as women in patriarchal societies, and second as persons with
disabilities in an inaccessible world. Key gender-related disability statistics include:
 Women with disabilities are more likely to experience gender-based violence than
non-disabled women.

 Women with disabilities have higher rates of unmet healthcare needs, including
reproductive health.
 In many countries, girls with disabilities are less likely to attend school.
 In India, female PwDs face compounded disadvantage in employment and marriage.
 WHO data shows women face unique barriers in rehabilitation service access.

CHAPTER 4: TYPES AND CLASSIFICATIONS OF
DISABILITY (WHO/ICF)
The WHO, through the International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health
(ICF), provides a comprehensive taxonomy of disability categories. These classifications
are used globally by health systems, policy makers, and researchers.
4.1 Physical / Locomotor Disabilities
Physical disabilities affect mobility, motor functions, and the overall ability to move and
perform physical tasks. They may be congenital or acquired through illness, injury, or
aging.
Locomotor Disability
Impairment of limbs or musculoskeletal system affecting movement. Includes conditions
such as paraplegia, quadriplegia, polio, cerebral palsy, clubfoot, and amputation. WHO
estimates over 450 million people have musculoskeletal conditions globally.

Cerebral Palsy
A group of non-progressive neurological disorders affecting movement, muscle tone, and
motor skills. Caused by damage to the brain before, during, or shortly after birth.
Prevalence: 2–3 per 1,000 live births globally.
Muscular Dystrophy
A group of genetic diseases causing progressive muscle weakness and degeneration.
Over 30 types are recognized. Duchenne MD is the most common, affecting approximately
1 in 3,500 male births.
Dwarfism
Characterized by short stature resulting from genetic or medical conditions.
Achondroplasia is the most common cause, occurring in approximately 1 in 25,000 births.
Acid Attack Injuries
Permanent physical disability resulting from intentional acid attacks. Disfiguration, vision
loss, and mobility impairments are common consequences.

4.2 Sensory Disabilities
Blindness
Complete loss of vision. Globally, approximately 43 million people are blind (WHO, 2019).
Leading causes include cataract (51%), glaucoma (8%), age-related macular degeneration
(5%), corneal opacity (4%), and diabetic retinopathy (1%). About 80% of global blindness
is avoidable.

Low Vision
Visual impairment not correctable to normal vision with standard glasses. Approximately
2.2 billion people have near or distance vision impairment, of whom at least 1 billion have
impairment that could have been prevented. Low vision includes tunnel vision, central
vision loss, and contrast sensitivity impairment.
Hearing Impairment (Deaf)
Profound hearing loss of 70dB or more in the better ear. WHO (2021) estimates 1.5 billion
people have some hearing loss, with 430 million having disabling hearing loss. By 2050,
over 700 million people may experience disabling hearing loss. Key causes: noise
exposure, infections, aging.
Hard of Hearing
Moderate to severe hearing loss (60–70 dB). These individuals can communicate through

spoken language with or without hearing aids. Early detection and intervention are critical
to language development in children.
Speech and Language Disability
Impairment in communication, language, or speech production. Includes stuttering,
aphasia, dysarthria, and voice disorders. Approximately 5% of children have a speech or
language disorder by age 8.
4.3 Intellectual and Developmental Disabilities
Intellectual Disability
Characterized by significant limitations in both intellectual functioning (reasoning, learning,
problem solving) and adaptive behavior. IQ below 70. Global prevalence estimated at
1–3%. Previously termed 'mental retardation'; now replaced with 'intellectual disability' in
most legislation including RPWD Act 2016.
Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD)

A developmental disorder affecting social interaction, communication, and behavior. WHO
estimates 1 in 100 children globally has autism. Recent US CDC data suggests 1 in 36
children. Male:female ratio is approximately 4:1.
Specific Learning Disabilities (SLD)
A heterogeneous group of disorders including dyslexia (reading), dyscalculia
(mathematics), and dysgraphia (writing). Affect 5–15% of school-age children globally. Not
related to intellectual ability.
Down Syndrome
A chromosomal condition caused by trisomy 21. Associated with intellectual disability and
physical features. Prevalence: approximately 1 in 1,000 live births globally (higher with

4.4 Mental Health Disabilities
WHO recognizes mental disorders as significant causes of disability. The Global Burden of
Disease study consistently ranks mental disorders among the leading causes of disability
worldwide. Depression is the single largest cause of disability globally.
Mental Illness
Includes substantial disorders of thinking, mood, perception, and behavior that impair
judgment and daily functioning. WHO (2022) estimates 1 in 8 people globally lives with a
mental disorder. Major types include depression, bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, and
anxiety disorders.
Depression
Affects 280 million people globally (WHO, 2021). Leading cause of disability worldwide.
Characterized by persistent sadness, loss of interest, sleep disturbance, and impaired

concentration. Treatable but grossly undertreated, especially in LMICs.
Schizophrenia
Affects approximately 24 million people globally (WHO). Characterized by hallucinations,
delusions, and disorganized thinking. Significantly impacts social and occupational
functioning. Major cause of psychiatric disability.
Bipolar Disorder
Affects approximately 45 million people globally. Characterized by alternating episodes of
mania and depression. Can cause severe disruption to work and relationships.
Anxiety Disorders
Most prevalent mental disorders globally. Affect approximately 301 million people
worldwide (WHO, 2019). Include generalized anxiety disorder, panic disorder, social
anxiety, PTSD, and OCD.
4.5 Neurological Disabilities
Multiple Sclerosis (MS)

A chronic, inflammatory, demyelinating disease of the central nervous system. Affects
approximately 2.8 million people worldwide. Can cause progressive disability including
mobility problems, visual disturbance, fatigue, and cognitive changes.
Parkinson's Disease
A progressive neurological disorder affecting movement, with tremors, rigidity, and
bradykinesia. Affects over 8.5 million people globally (WHO, 2022). Second most common
neurodegenerative disease after Alzheimer's.
Epilepsy

Characterized by recurrent seizures. Approximately 50 million people have epilepsy
globally (WHO). Around 80% live in low- and middle-income countries. A significant cause
of disability and social stigma.
Chronic Neurological Conditions
Includes a range of conditions such as motor neurone disease (ALS), Huntington's
disease, and other neurodegenerative disorders causing progressive physical and
cognitive disability.
4.6 Blood Disorders as Disabilities
India's RPWD Act 2016 uniquely includes three blood disorders as recognized disabilities,
reflecting the high burden of these conditions in the Indian population:
Haemophilia
An inherited bleeding disorder in which blood doesn't clot properly due to deficiency of

clotting factors (VIII or IX). Affects approximately 1 in 10,000 births globally. Can cause
spontaneous bleeding, joint damage, and chronic disability.
Thalassemia
A group of inherited blood disorders causing abnormal hemoglobin production. Over
300,000 affected births annually worldwide, with India having the highest burden. Severe
forms cause chronic anemia, organ damage, and developmental disability.
Sickle Cell Disease
An inherited disorder affecting the shape of red blood cells, causing pain crises, anemia,
and organ damage. Affects over 20 million people globally, with significant burden in tribal
populations across India.

CHAPTER 5: WHO – HEALTH INEQUITIES &
BARRIERS FACED BY PwDs

According to WHO, persons with disabilities face significant and systematic barriers to
accessing health services. These barriers manifest across multiple levels – from physical
inaccessibility of healthcare facilities to discriminatory attitudes of healthcare providers.

5.2 Types of Barriers (WHO Classification)
 Physical/Environmental Barriers: Inaccessible buildings, transport, information, and
communication. Lack of ramps, lifts, accessible toilets, and tactile pathways.
 Attitudinal Barriers: Negative stereotypes, stigma, discrimination, and low expectations
from family, community, employers, and healthcare providers.
 Institutional Barriers: Policies, procedures, and organizational structures that fail to
accommodate persons with disabilities. Absence of reasonable accommodation.
 Communication Barriers: Lack of sign language interpreters, Braille materials,
easy-read information, and accessible digital content.

 Financial Barriers: High cost of assistive devices, healthcare, and transport. Low
income due to employment exclusion creates a 'disability poverty trap.'
 Information Barriers: Lack of awareness among PwDs about their rights, available
services, and how to navigate health and social systems.
5.3 Disability and Poverty – The Vicious Cycle
WHO and World Bank data consistently show that disability and poverty are closely linked
in a self-reinforcing cycle. Disability can cause poverty through reduced employment,
increased medical costs, reduced productivity, and social exclusion. Conversely, poverty
can cause disability through poor nutrition, exposure to disease, unsafe living conditions,
and limited access to healthcare.

Key findings on disability and poverty:
→ In OECD countries, the employment rate of PwDs (44%) is barely half that of
non-disabled (75%).
→ People with disabilities earn 30–40% less than non-disabled workers in many
countries.
→ Households with a disabled member spend more on healthcare and support.
→ In India, 69% of PwDs reside in rural areas with minimal service access.
→ The 'extra cost of disability' (assistive devices, caregiving, transport) deepens poverty.

CHAPTER 6: WHO WORLD REPORT ON
DISABILITY – KEY CHAPTER FINDINGS
6.1 Rehabilitation Services
Rehabilitation is defined by WHO as 'a set of interventions designed to optimize functioning
and reduce disability in individuals with health conditions in interaction with their
environment.' Despite its critical importance, rehabilitation remains severely underfunded
and underutilized globally.
 An estimated 2.4 billion people currently need rehabilitation services globally (WHO,
2021).
 Only 1 in 3 people who need rehabilitation access it globally.
 In low-income countries, the gap is far worse – over 90% of needs remain unmet.
 Rehabilitation reduces hospitalization duration by 25–30% and improves functional
outcomes.

 Key rehabilitation services include physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy,
prosthetics and orthotics, and psychosocial rehabilitation.
6.2 Assistive Technology
Assistive technology (AT) enables persons with disabilities to live independent, productive
lives. WHO estimates that over 1 billion people need assistive technology, but fewer than
10% have access globally. This gap is particularly severe in low-income countries.

6.3 Education
Education is a fundamental right and a key determinant of life outcomes. However, children
with disabilities face significant barriers to education, despite progress in inclusive
education policies worldwide.
 Children with disabilities are less likely to attend school than non-disabled children in
every country studied by WHO.
 In Bolivia, 98% of non-disabled children attend school but under 40% of disabled
children do.

 In Indonesia, over 80% of non-disabled children attend school, but less than 25% of
disabled children do.
 Education completion gaps persist across all age groups, with the pattern more
pronounced in poorer countries.
 Globally, there are approximately 240 million children with disabilities; most lack
adequate educational support.
 Lack of trained teachers, accessible schools, and adapted curricula are the main
barriers.
6.4 Employment
Employment is critical for economic independence and social inclusion. However, persons
with disabilities face systematic exclusion from the labor market, both in formal and
informal sectors.
 In OECD countries, employment rate of PwDs is 44% vs 75% for non-disabled persons.

 In developing countries, the employment gap is wider, with many PwDs engaged only in
subsistence activities.
 Persons with intellectual, psychosocial, or multiple disabilities face the greatest
employment barriers.
 Workplace discrimination, inaccessible environments, and employer prejudice are major
barriers.
 Supported employment models have shown significant success in integrating PwDs into
competitive employment.

CHAPTER 7: WHO RECOMMENDATIONS AND
ACTION FRAMEWORK
7.1 WHO Global Disability Action Plan 2014–2021
The WHO Global Disability Action Plan (GDAP) 2014–2021 provided a framework for
countries to improve health and well-being for persons with disabilities. It was built on three
intersecting objectives:
 Objective 1: Remove barriers and improve access to health services and programmes.
 Objective 2: Strengthen and extend rehabilitation, habilitation, assistive technology,
assistance and support services, and community-based rehabilitation.
 Objective 3: Strengthen collection of relevant and internationally comparable data on
disability and support research on disability and related services.
7.2 Nine Cross-Cutting WHO Recommendations

The World Report on Disability (2011) highlighted nine cross-cutting recommendations
applicable to all sectors and governments:
1. Enable access to all mainstream systems and services.
2. Invest in specific programmes and services for people with disabilities.
3. Adopt a national disability strategy and plan of action.
4. Involve people with disabilities.
5. Improve human resource capacity.
6. Provide adequate funding and improve affordability.
7. Increase public awareness and understanding of disability.
8. Improve data collection on disability.
9. Strengthen and support research on disability.
7.3 WHO Rehabilitation 2030
In 2017, WHO launched the Rehabilitation 2030 initiative to address the growing global

unmet need for rehabilitation. The initiative calls for strong national rehabilitation systems
and sustainable rehabilitation service delivery integrated into health systems. Key
strategies include:
→ Strengthening leadership and governance for rehabilitation at national level.
→ Developing a sufficient, well-trained, and equitably distributed rehabilitation workforce.
→ Ensuring financing for rehabilitation through health insurance and social protection.

→ Integrating rehabilitation into all levels of health systems (primary, secondary, tertiary).
→ Implementing community-based rehabilitation (CBR) approaches.
→ Expanding access to quality assistive products in countries.

CHAPTER 8: INDIA'S DISABILITY LANDSCAPE –
CENSUS & DATA
8.1 Disability Prevalence in India
India is home to one of the largest populations of persons with disabilities in the world.
According to the Census of India 2011 – the most recent national census with disability
data – approximately 26.8 million people (2.21% of the total population) reported having
some form of disability. However, this figure is widely acknowledged to be a significant
underestimate, due to definitional limitations, stigma, lack of awareness, and non-inclusion
of many disability types.
Note: WHO estimates that disability prevalence in India could be as high as 10–20%,
consistent with global norms. The significant gap between the Census 2011 figure (2.21%)

and WHO estimates is a subject of ongoing academic and policy debate.

8.3 Disability in India – Key Policy Context
India's disability policy landscape has evolved significantly over the past three decades.
The key milestones include:

 1992: National Policy for Persons with Disabilities drafted.
 1995: Persons with Disabilities (Equal Opportunities, Protection of Rights and Full
Participation) Act enacted. Recognized 7 types of disability.
 1999: National Trust Act – for persons with autism, cerebral palsy, intellectual disability,
and multiple disabilities.
 2007: India ratified the UN Convention on Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UNCRPD).
 2012: National Policy on Disability finalized.
 2015: Accessible India Campaign (Sugamya Bharat Abhiyan) launched by Government
of India.
 2016: Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act (RPWD Act) enacted, replacing 1995 Act.
Expanded to 21 disability categories.
 2017: RPWD Act came into effect (April 19, 2017).

CHAPTER 9: RPWD ACT 2016 – OVERVIEW AND
BACKGROUND
9.1 Introduction to the RPWD Act 2016
The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 (RPWD Act / RPwD Act) is a landmark
piece of legislation enacted by the Indian Parliament on December 16, 2016. It received
Presidential assent on December 27, 2016, was notified in the Gazette of India on
December 28, 2016, and came into force on April 19, 2017. The Act is also officially
referred to as 'Act 49 of 2016.'
The RPWD Act replaces the earlier Persons with Disabilities (Equal Opportunities,
Protection of Rights and Full Participation) Act of 1995 (PwD Act, 1995), which was
considered inadequate and out of step with contemporary understandings of disability
rights.
9.2 Purpose and Objective

The primary objective of the RPWD Act 2016 is to give effect to the United Nations
Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UNCRPD, 2006), which India signed
on March 30, 2007, and ratified on October 1, 2007. The Act aims to:
✓ Ensure that every person with a disability can live their life with dignity, without
discrimination, and with equal opportunity.
✓ Promote, protect, and ensure the full and equal enjoyment of all human rights by
persons with disabilities.
✓ Promote respect for their inherent dignity.
✓ Ensure non-discrimination on the basis of disability.
✓ Enable full and effective participation in all spheres of society.
✓ Provide for reasonable accommodation in education and employment.
9.3 Structure of the Act

The RPWD Act 2016 is a comprehensive legislation consisting of 102 sections organized
across 17 chapters, plus a Schedule listing the 21 specified disabilities.

10.3 Detailed Profiles of Each Disability Under RPWD Act
1. BLINDNESS
Definition under RPWD Act: A condition where a person has any of the following
conditions: (a) total absence of sight; (b) visual acuity not exceeding 3/60 or 10/200
(Snellen) in the better eye with best possible spectacle correction; (c) limitation of field of
vision subtending an angle of less than 10 degrees.
Causes: Cataract (most common in India), glaucoma, diabetic retinopathy, trachoma,
retinitis pigmentosa, corneal opacity, vitamin A deficiency.
India Data: Over 12 million persons are blind in India. India accounts for nearly 20% of the
global blind population. National Programme for Control of Blindness (NPCB) is the key
government initiative.
2. LOW VISION

Definition: A condition where a person has any of the following conditions after best
correction: (a) visual acuity not exceeding 6/18 or less than 10/60 up to 3/60; (b) limitation
of field of vision between 10 and 40 degrees.
Low vision requires specialized rehabilitation including optical devices (magnifiers,
telescopes), non-optical aids, and environmental modifications. Unlike blindness, low
vision can often be functionally improved with proper aids.
3. LEPROSY CURED
Leprosy (Hansen's Disease) is caused by Mycobacterium leprae. Despite being cured
medically, many individuals retain permanent disabilities including clawing of fingers,
absorption of digits, foot drop, lagophthalmos (inability to close eyes), and skin changes.

India Context: India accounts for approximately 60% of global new leprosy cases annually.
The RPWD Act explicitly protects persons cured of leprosy from discrimination, a
significant step as they face severe social stigma.
4 & 5. HEARING IMPAIRMENT
Hearing Impairment in RPWD Act covers two sub-categories:  Deaf: 70 dB or more
hearing loss in the better ear in conversational range of frequencies.  Hard of Hearing:
60–70 dB hearing loss in the better ear.
Causes: Congenital (maternal rubella, birth asphyxia), infections (meningitis, chronic ear
infections), noise-induced, ototoxic drugs, aging.
India Data: ~50.7 lakh persons with hearing impairment (Census 2011). National
Programme for Prevention and Control of Deafness (NPPCD) provides free hearing aids.

6. SPEECH AND LANGUAGE DISABILITY
New in RPWD 2016. Defined as a permanent disability arising out of conditions such as
laryngectomy or aphasia affecting one or more components of speech and language due
to organic or neurological causes.
Types: Aphasia (language disorder after brain injury), dysarthria (motor speech disorder),
stuttering, voice disorders (dysphonia, laryngectomy).
This was added recognizing that inability to communicate effectively is a significant
disability impacting education, employment, and social participation.
7. LOCOMOTOR DISABILITY
Defined as a person's inability to execute distinctive activities associated with moving
oneself and objects resulting from affliction of musculoskeletal or nervous system.

India Census 2011: Largest disability group – ~54.36 lakh persons (20.3% of all PwDs).
Causes: Polio (historically major in India; now near-elimination post-eradication), paralysis,
stroke, spinal cord injury, accidents, congenital conditions, arthritis/bone disorders.
This category also includes partial or complete loss of limbs (amputation).
8. DWARFISM
Defined as a medical or genetic condition resulting in an adult height of 4 feet 10 inches
(147 cm) or less. More than 200 medical conditions can cause dwarfism. Achondroplasia is
the most common (approximately 70% of cases). Average height of a person with
achondroplasia is 4 feet (122 cm). The inclusion recognizes unique mobility, healthcare,
and social challenges.
9. INTELLECTUAL DISABILITY

Defined as a condition characterized by significant limitation in both intellectual functions
(reasoning, learning, problem solving) and adaptive behavior (conceptual, social and
practical skills) that originate before the age of 18 years.
Types under RPWD Act: (a) Specific intellectual disability; (b) Autism Spectrum Disorder.
Causes: Genetic (Down syndrome, Fragile X), prenatal infections, birth complications,
severe malnutrition, lead poisoning, meningitis.
India Data: ~15 lakh persons (Census 2011), significant underestimate.
10. SPECIFIC LEARNING DISABILITIES (SLD)

New in RPWD 2016. A heterogeneous group of conditions wherein there is a deficit in
processing language, spoken or written, that may manifest itself as a difficulty to
comprehend, speak, read, write, spell, or to do mathematical calculations and includes
such conditions as perceptual disabilities, dyslexia, dyscalculia, dysgraphia, dyspraxia, and
developmental aphasia.
Key Types: Dyslexia (reading/writing), Dyscalculia (math), Dysgraphia (writing), Dyspraxia
(movement coordination).
India: Estimated 10–15% of school children may have some form of SLD.
11. AUTISM SPECTRUM DISORDER (ASD)
A neuro-developmental condition characterized by challenges with social interaction,
communication, and restricted/repetitive behaviors. Spectrum ranges from mildly affected
to severely disabled.

RPWD Act recognizes ASD as a distinct disability eligible for all protections including
reservation in education and employment. This was a significant advance over the 1995
Act.
12. MENTAL ILLNESS
Defined as a substantial disorder of thinking, mood, perception, orientation, or memory that
grossly impairs judgment, behavior, capacity to recognize reality or ability to meet the
ordinary demands of life, but does not include retardation which is a condition of arrested
or incomplete development of mind.
Key conditions: Schizophrenia, major depression, bipolar disorder, severe anxiety
disorders, OCD, PTSD, personality disorders.
India: ~150 million people need active mental health interventions (NMHS 2015–16). Only
3–4 psychiatrists per million population (WHO norm: 10+).
13. CEREBRAL PALSY

A group of permanent disorders of the development of movement and posture, causing
activity limitations attributed to non-progressive disturbances in the developing fetal or
infant brain.
Types: Spastic (most common), Athetoid/Dyskinetic, Ataxic, Mixed.
India: Prevalence ~3 per 1,000 live births. Approximately 25 lakh children in India have
cerebral palsy. Closely linked to birth asphyxia (hypoxic ischaemic encephalopathy).
18. HAEMOPHILIA

An inherited bleeding disorder caused by deficiency of clotting factor VIII (Haemophilia A)
or factor IX (Haemophilia B). Causes spontaneous or trauma-induced bleeding into joints
and muscles, leading to chronic joint disease and locomotor disability.
India: ~1 in 10,000 males affected. The Haemophilia Federation of India estimates ~1.5
lakh individuals with haemophilia in India, though many are undiagnosed.
19. THALASSEMIA
An inherited hemoglobin disorder caused by mutations affecting globin chain production.
Beta-thalassemia major requires lifelong blood transfusions causing chronic disability.
India Context: India has the highest number of thalassemia major patients globally (~1 lakh
children). 10,000–15,000 children with thalassemia major born in India annually. An

estimated 4.4 crore Indians are carriers (thalassemia trait/minor).
20. SICKLE CELL DISEASE
An inherited hemoglobin disorder where red blood cells become sickle-shaped, causing
vaso-occlusive crises, severe pain, chronic anemia, and organ damage.
India: High prevalence in tribal populations of Central India (Maharashtra, MP, Odisha,
Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, Gujarat). National Mission on Sickle Cell Anaemia launched
2023. Estimated 20 million carriers and 2 lakh patients in India.
21. ACID ATTACK VICTIMS
Persons who are disfigured due to violent assaults by throwing of acid on their faces or
body. The RPWD Act 2016 uniquely includes acid attack victims recognizing the
permanent physical, sensory (vision loss, hearing loss), and psychological disabilities they
acquire.

India Context: India has one of the highest rates of acid attacks globally. 300+ cases
reported annually (National Crime Records Bureau), with significant underreporting.
Supreme Court has directed stricter regulation of acid sale.

CHAPTER 11: RPWD ACT 2016 – RIGHTS,
ENTITLEMENTS & PROVISIONS
11.1 Fundamental Rights Provided
 Right to Equality and Non-Discrimination (Sec. 3): No person with disability shall be
discriminated on ground of disability unless the act is a proportionate means of
achieving a legitimate aim. Women and children with disabilities shall have equal
rights.
 Right to Life with Dignity: Appropriate Government shall ensure PwDs enjoy right to
equality, life with dignity and respect for integrity equally with others.
 Protection from Cruelty and Inhuman Treatment: No PwD shall be subject to torture
or cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment. Protection from abuse, violence and
exploitation.
 Right to Community Inclusion: Appropriate Government to take steps to enable PwDs

to live in the community. This includes access to housing, social services, and support.
 Reproductive Rights: PwDs have right to decide freely the number, spacing, and timing
of children. Right to retain fertility. This was a landmark recognition in India.
 Right to Legal Capacity: PwDs have legal capacity on equal basis with others in all
aspects of life (Sec. 14). District Court may appoint limited guardian only as last resort.
 Right to Vote: Election Commission to ensure all polling stations are accessible.
Facilities for voting to be provided including assistance.
 Right to Own Property: PwDs have right to equal ownership of property and right to
inherit movable and immovable property.
11.2 Education – Key Provisions (Sec. 16–18)

→ Right to free and compulsory education for children with disabilities in neighborhood
school.
→ Special schools to be established for those who cannot be accommodated in
mainstream.
→ Reservation of seats in higher educational institutions (minimum 5% seats).
→ Scholarship to students with benchmark disabilities in higher education.
→ Appointment of 'Special Educators' in schools.
→ Accommodation in examinations – extra time, scribe, assistive devices.
→ Barrier-free accessible school buildings mandated.
→ Home-based education option for those unable to attend school.

→ Recognition and promotion of sign language; development of curriculum in sign
language.

11.4 Social Security and Healthcare

 PwDs with benchmark disability to be given insurance scheme (Sec. 24).
 Rehabilitation Council of India to maintain minimum standards in rehabilitation services.
 Early intervention services for children with disabilities.

 Reproductive healthcare and birth spacing for women with disabilities.
 National and State Funds for PwD welfare and financial assistance.

11.5 Accessibility Provisions (Sec. 40–46)
The RPWD Act places strong emphasis on accessibility as a prerequisite for inclusion.
These provisions align with the Accessible India Campaign (Sugamya Bharat Abhiyan)
launched by the Government of India in 2015.

→ Physical accessibility standards for all government buildings within prescribed
timeframe.
→ Accessible roads, transport, and infrastructure.
→ Accessible information and communication including websites, documents, and
signage.
→ All public and private service providers must comply with accessibility standards.
→ Access to judiciary – accessible courts and legal processes.
→ Development and implementation of Standards for Accessibility.

CHAPTER 12: RPWD ACT 2016 –
IMPLEMENTATION & CHALLENGES
12.1 Institutional Mechanisms
The RPWD Act establishes a multi-level institutional framework for implementation:
 Chief Commissioner for PwDs (National): Regulatory and grievance redressal body.
Monitors implementation; hears complaints from PwDs. To be supported by 2
Commissioners and an Advisory Committee.
 State Commissioners for PwDs: Equivalent body at the state level. Monitor state
implementation, address local grievances.
 District Level Committees: Address local concerns; constituted by State Governments.
 Central Advisory Board on Disability: Apex policy body with representatives from
ministries, experts, disabled persons' organizations.
 State Advisory Boards: Equivalent bodies at state level for policy guidance.

 National and State Funds: Financial mechanisms to support PwD welfare programs.
Existing funds subsumed under the new National Fund.
12.2 Unique Disability ID (UDID)
A major implementation initiative is the Unique Disability ID (UDID) card system, a national
database of persons with disabilities. UDID provides:
 Standardized disability certificate across the country.
 Digital database enabling tracking of benefits and services.
 Technology-enabled monitoring of implementation.
 Prevention of duplicate certificates and fraudulent claims.
 Smart card with embedded chip containing disability information.
12.3 Challenges and Gaps in Implementation
Despite the progressive provisions of the RPWD Act 2016, implementation has faced

numerous challenges. The Supreme Court of India, in Seema Girija Lal & Anr Vs Union of
India & Ors (2022–2023), expressed strong disappointment at inadequate implementation
across states including Andhra Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Uttar Pradesh, Punjab, Tripura, and
UT of Chandigarh.
■ Data Deficit: Census 2011 (the most recent national disability data) significantly
underestimates disability prevalence. New census and specialized disability survey
needed.

■ Rural-Urban Gap: 69% of PwDs reside in rural areas where services, assistive
devices, rehabilitation centers, and awareness are severely lacking.
■ Inadequate Funding: SIPDA (Scheme for Implementation of Persons with Disabilities
Act) budget increased by less than 9% over five years (2016–2021) despite growing
need.
■ Pension Inadequacy: Indira Gandhi National Disability Pension of Rs. 300–500/month
is grossly insufficient given inflation.
■ Limited Private Sector Compliance: Reservations in private sector employment are
voluntary. Most private employers do not implement accessibility or equal opportunity
policies.
■ State Inaction: Many states have not constituted District-Level Committees, Advisory
Boards, or State Funds as mandated by the Act.

■ Social Audit Gap: Section 48 mandating social audit of disability schemes has not
been implemented on priority basis.
■ Coordination Failure: Non-receipt of Utilization Certificates from states results in
non-release of central funds for disability initiatives.
■ Awareness Deficit: Majority of PwDs and their families are unaware of their rights,
entitlements, and available schemes under RPWD Act 2016.

CHAPTER 13: DISABILITY AND EDUCATION IN
INDIA
13.1 Education Policy and Disability
Education is both a fundamental right (Article 21A of the Constitution) and a key pathway
to social inclusion for persons with disabilities. India's disability-education landscape is
governed by multiple policy frameworks including the RPWD Act 2016, Right to Education
Act 2009, National Education Policy 2020 (NEP 2020), and Samagra Shiksha scheme.

13.3 National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 and Disability
The National Education Policy 2020 emphasizes inclusive education for children with
disabilities. Key provisions include:
 Foundational literacy and numeracy programs adapted for all learners including PwDs.
 National Curriculum Framework to include disability-inclusive content.
 Teacher education programs to include compulsory module on inclusive education.
 Universal Design for Learning (UDL) to be adopted across educational institutions.

 NIOS (National Institute of Open Schooling) to be strengthened for PwDs.
 Sign Language as a language of learning to be promoted.
 Assistive technology in education to be expanded.
13.4 Samagra Shiksha and Inclusive Education
Samagra Shiksha (2018) is the flagship integrated school education scheme that includes
a strong inclusive education component. Under Samagra Shiksha:
→ Assessment camps and Individualized Education Plans (IEPs) for children with
disabilities.
→ Resource rooms and home-based education programs.

→ Therapeutic and corrective equipment and aids to children with disabilities.
→ Orientation and mobility training; Braille kits for visually impaired.
→ Appointment of special educators in cluster resource centers.
→ Teacher sensitization and training on inclusive education.

14.2 Employment in India – RPWD Act Provisions and Reality
The RPWD Act 2016 mandates 4% reservation in government jobs for PwDs (up from 3%
under 1995 Act). Despite this legal provision, actual employment of PwDs in government
establishments remains far below targets.

 Only 34.4% of PwDs are 'main workers' vs 39.8% in general population (Census 2011).
 Employment opportunities in rural areas for PwDs are predominantly limited to
agriculture.
 Government has introduced National Career Service Centres (NCSCs) for PwDs.
 Vocational rehabilitation centers (VRCs) established under MSJE to provide training.
 NHFDC (National Handicapped Finance and Development Corporation) provides loans
for self-employment.
 Scheme for Rehabilitation of Persons with Disability (under DDRS) provides training.
 Corporate sector has begun to hire PwDs under ESG mandates – still early-stage.
 Mental illness and intellectual disability face the highest barriers to employment.
14.3 Supported Employment and Best Practices

International evidence strongly supports the 'supported employment' model as the most
effective approach to integrating PwDs – especially those with intellectual disabilities and
psychosocial conditions – into competitive employment.
 Job Matching: Careful assessment of individual's skills and interests matched to
appropriate jobs.
 Job Coach Support: Intensive on-site job coaching during initial employment period.

 Employer Sensitization: Training and support for employers to create inclusive
workplaces.
 Natural Supports: Building peer support networks in workplace.
 Follow-Along Support: Long-term, as-needed support after initial placement.

CHAPTER 15: CONCLUSION AND RESEARCH
GAPS
15.1 Summary of Key Findings
This comprehensive reference document has synthesized data from WHO global
publications and India's RPWD Act 2016 framework. The following key themes emerge:
✓ Disability is a universal human experience affecting approximately 1.3 billion people
globally (16% of the world's population).
✓ The WHO's biopsychosocial and human rights-based frameworks have fundamentally
transformed how disability is understood and addressed.
✓ India, with one of the world's largest disability populations, has made significant
legislative progress through the RPWD Act 2016.
✓ The expansion from 7 to 21 recognized disabilities under RPWD Act 2016 is a major
advancement aligned with international standards.

✓ Healthcare, education, and employment disparities for PwDs persist globally and in
India, requiring sustained multi-sectoral interventions.
✓ Implementation gaps – financial, institutional, and attitudinal – remain the primary
challenge in translating rights into reality.
✓ Data quality and availability on disability in India remain critical barriers to effective
policy and program monitoring.
15.2 Identified Research Gaps
For thesis purposes, the following research gaps represent significant opportunities for
original contribution to the disability studies field in India:
→ Up-to-date national disability prevalence data (Census 2011 is over a decade old; new
census data awaited).
→ Impact evaluation of RPWD Act 2016 on actual outcomes for PwDs across the 21
disability categories.

→ Comparative analysis of implementation across different states – gaps and success
factors.
→ Specific outcome data on newly recognized disabilities (SLD, ASD, blood disorders)
under RPWD.
→ Gender-disaggregated analysis of RPWD Act implementation – women with
disabilities.
→ Effectiveness of UDID (Unique Disability ID) system in service delivery.

→ Mental health and psychosocial disability – the most neglected category under RPWD
implementation.
→ Intersectionality of disability with caste, class, and geography in India.
→ Private sector compliance with RPWD Act provisions and reasons for low adoption.
→ Community-based rehabilitation effectiveness in rural Indian settings.
15.3 Policy Recommendations
 Conduct a dedicated National Disability Survey to update prevalence data (not
dependent on census).
 Implement RPWD Act Section 48 – mandatory social audit of all disability schemes.
 Increase SIPDA budget allocation commensurate with scale of disability burden.
 Strengthen National and State Advisory Boards on Disability with time-bound action
plans.

 Expand rural disability services through integrated community health and rehabilitation
workers.
 Mandate disability-disaggregated data collection in all government schemes and reports.
 Develop awareness campaigns targeting families, communities, employers, and
healthcare providers.
 Introduce disability-competency training in all healthcare professional curricula.
 Enforce accessibility standards through third-party audits and public reporting.
 Establish a National Disability Data Observatory for real-time monitoring.

APPENDIX A: CASE STUDIES IN DISABILITY
INCLUSION
A.1 Case Study: Disability Inclusion in Primary Healthcare –
Kerala
Kerala is widely regarded as India's best-performing state in disability inclusion, driven by
its high literacy, strong public health infrastructure, and progressive welfare policies. The
Kerala Social Security Mission (KSSM) and Kerala Viklangam Welfare Corporation have
been instrumental in implementing disability rights.
Key Features of Kerala's Disability Inclusion Model:
 Comprehensive disability database maintained at Panchayat level.
 Door-to-door identification and enrollment of PwDs in welfare schemes.
 Kanivu – a rehabilitation scheme providing free assistive devices and therapy.
 Accessible healthcare facilities across all government hospitals.

 Disability pension linked to Aadhaar for direct benefit transfer.
 Special schools and rehabilitation centers in every district.
 Strong disabled persons' organizations (DPOs) participating in governance.

A.2 Case Study: Autism Spectrum Disorder – Policy and Practice
in India

ASD was first formally recognized in India through the National Trust Act 1999, which
included autism among its four target disabilities alongside cerebral palsy, intellectual
disability, and multiple disabilities. Its inclusion in the RPWD Act 2016 further strengthened
legal protections.
→ India has no national epidemiological survey on ASD prevalence. Small-scale studies
suggest 1–1.5% prevalence.

→ Services for ASD are concentrated in urban areas and private sector, creating access
inequities.
→ National Trust provides SAMARTH scheme – early intervention and respite care for
children with ASD.
→ Government has established National Institute of Mental Health & Neurosciences
(NIMHANS) as a national referral center.
→ RPWD Act 2016 guarantees education, employment, and social security for persons
with ASD.
→ Research gap: Population-based ASD survey in India urgently needed.
→ Parent organizations like Action for Autism and AAPC play critical advocacy roles.
A.3 Case Study: Thalassemia – India's Blood Disorder Challenge
India faces the world's highest burden of thalassemia, making its inclusion in RPWD Act

2016 particularly significant. The human, economic, and social impact of thalassemia in
India underscores the need for integrated disability, prevention, and treatment policies.
 India has approximately 100,000 children living with thalassemia major.
 10,000–15,000 new thalassemia major children are born each year in India.
 Approximately 4.4 crore (44 million) Indians carry the thalassemia trait – the highest
globally.
 Regular blood transfusions (every 2–4 weeks) are required for thalassemia major
patients.
 Bone marrow transplant is the only cure but is expensive (Rs. 10–15 lakh) and not
universally accessible.
 Chelation therapy (iron removal) is required alongside transfusions, adding to cost
burden.

 RPWD Act recognition means thalassemia patients now have access to 4% reservation
in government employment.
 Thalassemia India and Thalassemia International Federation advocate for universal
neonatal screening.

APPENDIX D: GLOSSARY OF KEY TERMS
Accessibility: The degree to which an environment, product, or service is available and
usable by people with disabilities without barriers. Governed by universal design principles.
Adaptive Behavior: Conceptual, social, and practical skills learned and performed by
people in everyday life. Used in diagnosing intellectual disability.
Assistive Technology (AT): Any device, software, or equipment that helps people with
disabilities perform functions that might otherwise be difficult or impossible.
Benchmark Disability: Under RPWD Act 2016 – a person with not less than 40% of a
specified disability as certified by a medical authority.
Community-Based Rehabilitation (CBR): A strategy within community development for

rehabilitation, equalization of opportunities, poverty reduction, and social inclusion of
people with disabilities.
Disability: Under RPWD Act 2016 – a person with long-term physical, mental, intellectual
or sensory impairment which, in interaction with barriers, hinders full and effective
participation in society equally with others.
Disabled Persons Organizations (DPOs): Organizations controlled by persons with
disabilities (at least 51% of membership) advocating for disability rights and inclusion.
Durable Functional Limitation: A long-term or permanent limitation in physical, mental, or
sensory functioning.
Equal Opportunity Policy (EOP): A document required from all government
establishments under RPWD Act specifying measures for employment of PwDs.

Habilitation: Helping a person with disability develop skills and abilities they were never
able to perform (vs. rehabilitation which restores abilities).
ICF (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health): WHO's
universal framework for describing health and disability, using a biopsychosocial approach.
Inclusion: The process of improving the terms of participation in society for people who
are disadvantaged. In disability – ensuring PwDs are part of mainstream society.
Locomotor Disability: Disability resulting from affliction of musculoskeletal or nervous
system affecting movement, strength, endurance, mobility, or coordination.
Participation Restriction: Problems a person with disability may experience when
involved in life situations.

Reasonable Accommodation: Necessary and appropriate modifications to ensure
persons with disabilities can enjoy equal rights on an equal basis.

Rehabilitation: A set of interventions designed to optimize functioning and reduce
disability in individuals with health conditions in interaction with their environment.
RPWD Act 2016: Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act 2016 (Act 49 of 2016) – India's
landmark disability legislation enacted December 2016, in force April 2017.
Specified Disability: One of the 21 disabilities listed in the Schedule to the RPWD Act
2016 that are legally recognized disabilities in India.
UDID: Unique Disability Identity Card – a government-issued digital disability certificate in
India under the Swavalamban Card scheme.
UNCRPD: United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (2006) –
the international human rights treaty on disability rights.

Universal Design: Design of products, environments, programs, and services usable by
all people to the greatest extent possible, without need for adaptation.
